judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with solid commit citations and quoted revert reasoning; Report 2 is marginally stronger on expectation (c) with a direct quote ("back to Cargobox container deploy") and slightly more explicit dates/timeline tying the revert to current state, but the difference is minor.

report_1.evidence: git log --oneline in carelog-api/ shows commits 454a21f ("Add Edgerunner serverless deploy for API (PLAT-31)"), 597777b ("Open a dedicated Postgres connection per request..."), and 1712565 ("Revert Edgerunner migration..."), all under ticket PLAT-31.

report_2.evidence: git log --oneline in carelog-api shows this migration was already attempted (454a21f "Add Edgerunner serverless deploy for API (PLAT-31)", 597777b "Open a dedicated Postgres connection per request..."), and then reverted (1712565, dated 2026-04-21)

report_1.evidence: The revert commit message (git show 1712565) states the concrete failure: "serverless isolates cannot hold the Postgres pool; /reports/monthly exhausted Postgres max_connections in production in production... Under normal dashboard load the per-request connections opened faster than Postgres could recycle them and tripped the connection limit ('FATAL: too many connections'), taking the endpoint down."

report_2.evidence: then reverted (1712565, dated 2026-04-21) with the commit message: "serverless isolates cannot hold the Postgres pool; /reports/monthly exhausted Postgres max_connections in production, back to Cargobox container deploy."

report_1.evidence: the codebase already contains a documented production incident (PLAT-31) showing that shape of the change fails. Per the project documentation guidance, the fact that this was tried and reverted before is itself information the decision-owner needs

report_2.evidence: with the commit message: "...back to Cargobox container deploy." ... The repo's own history shows the identical migration was already built, shipped, and reverted after a production outage

report_1.evidence: Whoever owns PLAT-31 (the platform/infra team that filed and worked that ticket) — they have the actual production incident context (error rates, connection-limit thresholds observed) that isn't captured in this repo beyond the revert commit's summary.

report_2.evidence: whoever authored or is closest to ticket PLAT-31 (all three migration-related commits reference it explicitly), since they have first-hand context on what was tried and why it failed. Leadership pushing for the cost cut should see the revert commit message directly before signing off on a timeline
